title: U.S. v. Alameda County Veterinary Medical Association
text:
ANTHONY E. DESMOND

MARK F. ANDERSON
2 CHRIS'rOPllER S CROOK

7
8 UNITED STATES DISTRICT COURT
9 NORTHERN DISTRICT OF CALIFORNIA
UNITED STATES OF AMERICA,
Civil No. 75-2398-CVR
11 Plaintiff,
COMPLAINT
v.
12
u.s.c.
15 § 1
13 ALAMEDA COUNTY VETERINARY (Sherman Antitrust Act)
MEDICAL ASSOCIATION,
14 Filed:
November. lL~, 1975
Defendant.
15
16 The United States of America, plaintiff, by its
attorneys, acting under the direction of the Attorney
18 General of the United States, brings this civil action
19 against the above-named defendant, and complains and
alleges as follows:
21 I
22 JURISDICTION AND VENUE
1. This complaint is filed and proceedings are
instituted under Section 4 of the Act of Congress of
July 2, 1890, as amended (15 U.S.C. §4), commonly known as
26 the Sherman Act, in order to prevent and restrain the
27 continuing violation by the defendant, as hereinafter
u.s.c.
alleged, of Section 1 of said Act (15 §1).
29 2. The defendant maintains an office, transacts
business and is found within the Northern District of
31 California.
32
o -

II
THE DEFENDJ\NT
3. The Alameda County Veterinary Medical Association
(hereinafter referred to as ALVMA) is made the defendant
herein. ALVMA is a non-profit corporation organized under
the laws of the State of California and having its princi-
pal place of business in Oakland, California. ALVMA is an
association of doctors of veterinary medicine engaged in
the business of providing veterinary services.
4. At the present time, over 100 veterinarians are
members of ALVMA. Almost all the ALVMA members practice
within the County of Alameda, State of California.
III
CO-CONSPIRATORS
5. Various members and officers of ALVMA, not named
as defendants in this complaint participated as co-
conspirators in the violation hereinafter alleged and have
performed acts dnd made statements in thereof.
f~rtherance
IV
TRADE AND COMMERCE
6. Members of ALVMA are doctors of veterinary medicine
who provide veterinary services for animal owners located in
the County of Alameda, State of California, and surrounding
areas. Members of ALVMA are licensed to practice
veterinary medicine by the State of California.
7. ALVMA members in· their veterinary practices
realize gross revenues in excess of $5 million per year.
8. ALVMA members necessarily use anesthetics, anti-
biotics, vaccines, serums, other drugs and surgical supplies
in providing veterinary services. In doing so, ALVMA
members each purchase and resell to their customers those
drug and surgical items.

9. During the period of time covered by this complaint,
substantially all of the anesthetics, antibiotics, vaccines,
serums, other drugs and surgical supplies used by ALVMA
'
members were produced in states other than California and
were shipped to California from the various states of
manufacture. There was a substantial and continuous flow
in interstate commerce of such anesthetics, antibiotics,
vaccines, serums, other drugs and surgical supplies from
suppliers located outside of California directly and indirect-
ly through wholesalers to ALVMA members and thence to their
customers.
10. During the period of time covered by this complaint,
the activities of the defendant and its members, as
described herein, were performed within the flow of interstate
commerce, and are an essential and integral part of that flow.
Said activities had a substantial effect upon the aforesaid
interstate commerce.
v
VIOLATION ALLEGED
11. Beg~nning at least as early as 1967 and continuing
to the date of the filing of this complaint, the defendant
and the co-conspirators have engaged in a combination and
conspiracy in unreasonable restraint of the aforesaid
trade and commerce in violation of Section 1 of the
u.s.c.
Sherman Act, as amended (15 §1). Said unlawful
combination and conspiracy is continuing and will continue
unless the relief hereinafter prayed for is granted.
12. The aforesaid combination and conspiracy has
consisted of a continuing agreement and concert of action
among the defendant and co-conspirators to raise, fix
stabilize, and maintain fees charged by members of the
defendant for rendering veterinary services.

13. In effectuating the aforesaid combination and
conspiracy, the defendant and co-conspirators ·have done the
things which, as hereinbefore alleged, they agreed and
conspired to do, including, among other things, the following:
(a) conducted fee surveys;
(b) published results of fee surveys;
(c) adopted minimum fee schedules;
(d) adopted suggested fee schedules;
(e) published, circulated and utilized
minimum and suggested fee schedules; and
(f) prohibited ALVMA members from accepting
referrals from animal welfare agencies for
spay and neuter operations at reduced fees.
VI
EFFECTS
14. The aforesaid combination and conspiracy has had
he following effects, among others:
(a) Fees for veterinary services of members
of defendant have been raised, fixed,
stabilized, and maintained at artificial
. and non-competitive levels;
(b) Price competition among ALVMA members
in the sale of veterinary services has
been suppressed and restrained; and
(c) Animal owners requiring the services
offered by members of ALVMA have been
deprived of the benefits of free and
open competition in the sale of such
services.

PRAYER
WHEREFORE, plaintiff prays:
1. That the Court adjudge and decree that the defendant
and co-conspirators have engaged in an unlawful combination and
conspiracy in restraint of the aforesaid trade and conunerce in
iolation of Section 1 of the Sherman Act.
2. That ·the defendant, its officers, directors, agents,
employees, and successors and all other persons acting or
claiming to act on its behalf be enjoined and restrained from,
in any manner, directly or indirectly, continuing, maintaining,
r renewing the combination and conspiracy hereinbefore alleged,
r from engaging in any other combination, conspiracy, contract,
understanding, or concert of action having a similar
effect, and from adopting or following any practice,
lan, program, or device having a similar purpose or effect.
3. That the defendant be directed to furnish the text
any final judgment entered in this case to each of its
4. That the plaintiff have such other and further relief
s the nature of the case may require and the Court may deem
and proper.
s.
That the plaintiff recover its taxable costs.
THOMAS E. KAUPER MARK F. ANDERSON
